speaker: interviewer
text: It's Tuesday, November the 28th, 1995. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Wlodka Robertson. We're in London, England. And this is tape three. So you are now with the Seraphin, the second Seraphin family. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what-- what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was winter, and it became obvious that the Germans were going to lose the war. We could see the Germans once again. This was already-- in-- [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know exactly where it was also in connection with Warsaw. But anyway, we could see that the Germans were retreating. And then suddenly one day the Russians were there. And they came in also looking terribly poor and bedraggled, and came-- I remember came through this cottage and were asking for food. And the people were saying they don't have any food. And they didn't have any food. I remember the Russian soldiers saying, oh, well, if you really don't have any food, we'll have to give you ours. I remember that. And-- and then this Jan Seraphin, Jan Seraphin was saying to me that he would never have to worry about again. He would get a lot of money from my family. And I was saying-- I was agreeing with him. I wasn't-- I didn't even know if my father was alive at that time. And-- but I didn't mention that-- because I began to tell this story how-- how Seraphins went and collected stuff from Warsaw and sold it at the market and by chance met [INAUDIBLE] and her husband, at this market. And [INAUDIBLE] and her husband, came to see me, still when the Germans were occupying Poland. And-- and also they very much wanted to know what happened to his parents and their little sister, because I was-- during the uprising, I remember I said so in the same building with her. But I didn't know what happened to them. And so I-- I was-- I felt bad about that. And they-- they went away. And especially about the little girl, they wanted to know. And I didn't know what happened to her. But I knew that they knew where I was once the Russians came. So I didn't really do anything, didn't-- didn't sort of think of getting-- trying to find anybody, or who will I find. I had no shoes. So I waited. But eventually, we-- the Seraphins somehow decided to go to Warsaw and found some hole, some-- in the ruins. And I went with them. And still didn't-- I thought I was the only kind of Jew around. That I was-- [INAUDIBLE]. I still wasn't going out. It was winter. I didn't have any shoes. And then one day-- and the Russians had already come.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was February '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. [? Ala, ?] who was-- who then became wife of [INAUDIBLE], she suddenly knocked on the door. I didn't know her, but she was a young woman in her early 20s. And she came to collect me. [LAUGHS] So that's [INAUDIBLE]. I also remember [INAUDIBLE]. I had thought that no one was alive, that perhaps [INAUDIBLE] was not alive, and-- and wasn't sure what to do. And then [? Ala ?] suddenly came. She had gone to the village and they knew where we had to go to Warsaw. And she came.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was really the end of the war for me. She told me she knew where my sister was. And she took me to some old friends of my parents who were now living in Lodz. Which was quite-- quite a journey from where you were or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not terribly far by train. We went probably, I don't know, two hours probably. And-- so from my point-- my point of view, that was-- the end-- the end of all those happenings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister also came out to there too?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister found herself. Yes, she was more enterprising. She and Mrs. Dubil-- because she stayed with the same family all along-- they walked the streets of Warsaw until they recognized some people as Jews, because some people came back from Russia. And they were much more openly. They were not so shocked. Or-- I think they were much more visible as Jews, speaking Yiddish, perhaps. And then they asked if they knew there was some sort of committee. And they were told the [INAUDIBLE] of Jewish help committee. And they went there [INAUDIBLE]. And when they went in, they-- the first person they saw was [INAUDIBLE] working there. And she knew that there was this family-- this family who were friends of my parents, who are now in Lodz. And we were just-- that was arranged that we would go and stay with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you met up with your sister there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I met up there. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soon after that was a knock on the door. And my cousin, who's about six years or seven years older than I, that we stayed in the ghetto and hid together. He survived and his little brother. And he somehow knew about this family who are called [INAUDIBLE], who were-- he thought that they may know what had happened to me and my sister. So he came to ask them. And when he came, they were-- we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what your sort of reaction was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- that was wonderful years because we shared all that life in the ghetto together and lived together in this one little room in one bunker and one bed sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about-- you were 14, because you met them at this time, your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my little cousin-- yes, he's three years younger. So he was about 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your-- your sister had been with the Dubils the whole time, but they presumably had had to move out of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I don't even remember or know very much exactly where they went. Also somewhere outside Warsaw and then came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in-- in Lodz for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It was also probably about three or four months, because my father came to England during the war in '42 or '43, I think, from Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And yes, somehow he found contact with the [INAUDIBLE] and so on. So quite quickly he made the connection. And he began to arrange for me and my sister to come to London. And we did. We came. It must have been very few months afterwards. But in the meanwhile while I was in Poland, we stayed in Lodz. And then I went to-- I was ill with typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which you got then at the end of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was the other typhus, the stomach typhus, not the lice typhus. And then somehow went, stayed in a children's home ran for Jewish children. And yes, I was-- had been there a few because I met some people my age who I made friends with. And also had to escort because I was just for a few weeks. They-- some of the older children went to the local school. And I sort of looked after the little children, quite important. So I-- and we had to protect them from the children in the village, the little children who went to school were kind of [INAUDIBLE] sometimes. I and this other boy who didn't go to school used to go and try to protect them from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what your sort of state of mind was at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think a bit of a shock really. That's why, oh yes, [INAUDIBLE] lethargic. I just did what I was told, you know, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was all rather sort of unbelievable to you in-- in the way what had-- everything that happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I sort of really didn't feel in control because all that had happened. I wasn't-- I didn't in any way arrange it. It all somehow happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father arranged for you and your sister to go England. Did he come to Lodz? I mean, did you see him before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't come to Poland. He had all his experiences in Russia. And and was very wary of coming to-- to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you get to London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Someone who my father knew from New York, from the Jewish Labor Committee, was coming to Warsaw, bringing some aid. And we traveled with him to Sweden first because he happened to be going to Sweden, and then came to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by train to Sweden was it? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, by plane-- by plane, and then by plane here to Croydon Airport. And then by bus to somewhere to Central London where my father was waiting for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did seem a stranger. I had these romantic memories of him. He was quite-- but yes, I remember calling him [? Pom, ?] but which is the sort of mister at first by mistake. But no, he didn't seem to have changed so much, just that I hadn't seen him through all these happenings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard to sort of reacquaint yourself with him in a way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. In-- but yes, probably, that six years had made it, you know made a little difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I guess you and your sister had probably got back--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, we became very dependent on each other. And my father, he had a woman friend who became his wife. And I think she had quite a hard time with us because we resented her at first and we-- there were two of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had already remarried by the time you got to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't remarry. But this lady called Barbara, she was around.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was difficult for you not having-- your own mother wasn't there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I think we kind of-- we had these visions of looking after my father and so on, and were rather disappointed to see that he [LAUGHS] didn't really need that. He was a busy, busy man doing all sorts of other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had already found someone to look after himself. Was he still connected with political things?

words.text: He had already found someone to look after

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. For-- well, he had connections with the Bund for-- well, always, but not so actively unless, I suppose, as time went out. But he was very active in the Labour Party here and felt it was his mission to tell them about what happening in Russia during the war and so on, was busy [INAUDIBLE] some books.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me a bit about your sort of your family life over in England when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well we came and stayed with my father and Barbara, my stepmother. You know, she moved in. At first she wasn't there. And went-- went-- went to school at the local grammar school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where-- this was around Croydon? Or-- no--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we lived in Finsbury Park. Lived in Finsbury Park. And I went to school to Stamford Hill, small school there.

speaker: interviewer
text: A very Jewish area that you were living in Stamford Hill?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stamford Hill is very Jewish, but Finsbury Park is not as far as I know. And-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yes, I think we knew some people, young people, who came here from Poland and make some close connections with them, met them quite often. And there was even a little sort of group that thought they were some little Bundist group because we knew some people who was of children of this people. So that made it easier to learn the English.

speaker: interviewer
text: You learned English by going to-- from school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I went through some teacher for about six weeks before, because we came here in the summer, I think. And then the rest, yes, then school. What else was I just thinking about at that time? Yes, I remember how London still seemed-- we went to Sweden first and stayed for a week. That was wonderful. Sweden seemed full of lights and oranges. And London was still so blacked out and cold. And the houses were so cold. And there were rations. So it seemed quite somber at that time. And then my father and my [INAUDIBLE] moved to Muswell Hill. But we still went to the  same school, to Stamford Hill.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is-- was your experience, that-- it was not too difficult coming from Europe into England at that time? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean what from-- because-- sort of having the passports and things, or just the change?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, just being accepted and integrating into--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- yes. Well, I wasn't used to being integrated. It was so difficult to be in Poland and just little school there and being shouted at and so on. I think I-- yes, comparatively, it was certainly not more difficult. It was plainly easier here. And I had my family who did look after me. And-- I still I think I was probably a little bit-- you know, shocked in some ways. But I seem to have managed at school, seem to have passed my exams.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father seemed happy to be in England did he?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was very happy to be in England, yes. I think, yes, he really liked England very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so tell me what sort of-- you studied at school here and went on. Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, yes-- I passed my exams and then went to do radiography at the Royal Free Hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that something you'd had a long interest in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the opposite. But-- [LAUGHS] but I found myself doing science, which I wasn't particularly good. But I was-- I thought, well, perhaps vaguely I was thinking I could perhaps do medicine or something like that, because I thought with my accent and so on it probably wasn't a very good idea to do things with language-- I don't know. Anyway, I did some science. And then it sort of became obvious that there weren't that many things I could do. But I went for some interview. I saw a radiography advertised [INAUDIBLE], told me to join that school. And yes, it wasn't a sort of natural thing that I particularly good at. But I was still very useful. I worked in radiography most of my working time. My sister married very, very young. We went to Belgium to some camp where there were people from camps. And some Bundist group of people. And she met [? Carl ?] when she was 17 or 18 and then went, yes, went to stay in Belgium with him, and then came with him to London. And they had a baby very soon. But he was a printer by training. And he-- those problems with the unions, he couldn't get jobs in sort of union places and worked in some little Jewish printing shop and earned very little money. And eventually-- and he had brothers in America. So they went to arrange for them to move to America. So I was very sad that she went. But by that time, I knew Bruce. I knew Bruce since I was-- that's my husband-- since I was about 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had you met?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him in the Labour Party, in the-- which I-- sort of youths section of the Labour Party. He-- so that made, yes, life easy. I sort of moved out of my father's house and then got married when I was 23. I knew Bruce for about six years before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew all through the time you were studying. You were sort of going out with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, since I was 17, yes. And I-- yes. And we then lived in a flat. And then I was expecting a baby. We went to live in [INAUDIBLE], a new town. Bruce was working at first in a wool secretariat and then for the Labour Party in the research department there. And then I [INAUDIBLE]. Then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we went-- we went to France. He worked at the OECD and stayed there for almost four years, when my son was born. So I had two daughters when we went. And then my son was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we lived in Paris. So that was quite a holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: You enjoyed that time, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- it took me, yes, a year to get used to it. I didn't-- I didn't know any French because I did Polish for my exams, not French at all. And so it took a year to make it. And I had very small children and had the baby and so on. So I didn't find it-- but after that first year, yes, I liked it. It was quite an easy life. And then we came to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- you were working as a-- you worked as a radiographer while your children were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked as a radiographer until my first child was born. Then I didn't work when I had two small children. Then we went to France. I didn't-- but as soon as we-- but then as soon as my youngest child was five and went to school, I began to work sort of part time. We also got jobs locally in small hospitals.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what have been your interests since being here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose I've always been interested in politics. It's a sort of past time. And we've traveled quite a lot. Luckily we had the opportunity. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I suppose these are my main interests.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about your children, what they've gone on to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have three children. And eldest daughter, Isabel, she works in housing for her. She has worked in different housing associations, and so on. It seems she has probably particularly successful at school, she was-- and has found quite easy to do whatever she wants to do. And she's now living in Liverpool, her husband and their little daughter working, sort of, job sharing. And he's a civil servant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- she has been here just last weekend. We do see them quite often. And my younger daughter lives in Newcastle. She has two little boys. She lives with her partner called Pat. And she works at Sunderland University in the administration there. And she seems to cope with her life very well. She seems happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son, he-- [LAUGHS] because they went to university, yes. The eldest daughter went to Sussex. And Susie, the younger one, went to Canterbury. And Mark, who went to local comprehensive school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then went on to Dartington. He become very interested in theater and got a degree there. And since then he's been doing different things with the theater, community theater. He just now has a part time job within The Young Vic, trying to set up a  youth theater. So, yes, that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk with them about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found for years I was-- I couldn't when they were younger. But yes, it's become much more easier. They seem very interested. And I know Isabel has lots of books about Holocaust. And I know Mark's girlfriend was asking me for-- for different material to talk to the children. She was a teacher. And Susie too is interested. And we went to Poland very recently-- and this was the third time we went to Poland-- to put a plaque in the Jewish cemetery for families. We did that in-- we talked about this with my cousins in France and decided to put on plaque for the whole three families. And Isabel came for a few days as well. This was her first time to Warsaw, to sort of see this little ceremony. And so did my cousin's daughter. They didn't come. The cousins of mine didn't come. But Bruce and I went, and Isabel, and the other young woman also called Isabel. So I happy that-- I think it was Bruce mainly who-- who kind of managed to organize it. I don't think I would have organized it, the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a child, your life, while you were not religious, there was-- you had a very Jewish cultural life. And all of your friends were. Obviously in England that experience has been very different for you. Has not been a conscious, deliberate choice, or is that just the way things have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think it was a choice. And in some ways, I can see that my sister's life in New York where she lives among this Jewish community seems cozy and easier. But it was because I met Bruce. And that seems to have worked out. It has. And-- so-- that's not a life I-- I can-- I can have. But it wasn't on purpose. No, it wasn't a choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you been involved in Jewish sort of Holocaust activism-- sort of groups since? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I belong to the Holocaust-- Holocaust Survivor Center in Hendon. Must be about three years ago that it was opened. And yes, I found that useful. So it's a long way from here. And in some ways, I find I know go less because it's so far. And it's likely that the people who live very nearby are so much more part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a particular group that you went to there, particular thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I now go. And I like going to a group, sort of support group of the hidden children. And there is a small people-- group of people. And we've got to like each other. And that's-- I got about every three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you talk, you sort of share things about your past experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and it's quite useful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we finish, is there anything that we've missed out that you-- that you feel you want to say or if there's any kind of message that you'd like to sort of hand on?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the things I was thinking about was that there is this idea that the Jews in some ways were different from other people and let themselves be killed. And it's just not my experience. I mean, I saw a lot of resistance. And the people-- but-- and I have seen other people in similar situations that are surrounded by soldiers with guns and they did no more, no less than the Jews that I saw. And I would like to say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: That there was-- there were people fighting and rebelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: That people did as much as other people. I think that they were not-- there wasn't-- [INAUDIBLE] all these uprisings in-- in-- in death camps and many of them. So that's one thing. On the other hand, just trying not to kind of-- I know this is terrible old feeling, among the Poles and the Jews, but maybe, I don't know, 50 years later, maybe I shouldn't-- should try to remember the better things about it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because I-- for years, I didn't remember all the terrible things. Yeah.

words.text: That people did as much as

speaker: interviewer
text: Really want a more balanced--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, perhaps that's not completely fair.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, is there anything else you'd like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think that's-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you very much, indeed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for doing this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wlodka, would you like to introduce your husband to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is Bruce, my husband. And we've known each other I think-- well, we've been married for 40 years, so it must be about 45 years, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, since 1951 I think. We met in the Labour Party League of the Youth, didn't we? [LAUGHS] When I-- shortly after I went to university.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what attracted you to-- to to Wlodka?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very interesting question.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, that's very difficult to say. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, we-- she had a common political interest, I think. And with her sense of humor as much as anything else, I think she laughed at my jokes. [LAUGHS] And I laughed at hers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you still laughing at each other's jokes after 40 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, yes. Yes, sometimes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you talk much about Wlodka's experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't think we did in those days. I knew-- well, I knew her father of course. I knew more about her father's background really. Then I knew-- I knew roughly Wlodka's history. But she didn't talk much about it in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's more recently that she's about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm. Mm. I've-- I've learned more in the past few years, I think, than I knew for many years at the beginning of our marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say, either of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it seems a happy ending on the whole, for me, anyway. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: It's been interesting for me. We went to Poland last summer. We've-- I've been once before, but last summer was more interesting the way we visited various places where Wlodka had been, including the village outside Warsaw where she spent the last winter of the war. And we tried to find the family or people whom she knew. And though we didn't succeed, it was interesting to see where she had lived during that time. And also we went around the various sites of the ghetto in Warsaw and saw the various monuments. And we, of course, put up the plaque in the Jewish cemetery, which I found very moving, the whole place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was saying that you-- but for you, I wouldn't have organized this. But you were very insistent and diligent in organizing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's probably an important thing to have done as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somewhat, yes. Done it. And we seemed to have-- our children have grown up to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: Succeeding at doing what they want to do on their own, and seem to cope with their lives well.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must be proud of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite happy, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think these interviews that you're giving-- Wlodka's giving will be of very great interest to our children, and perhaps even to our grandchildren, [LAUGHS] in the course of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is earliest picture that I still have. It's-- my uncle in America had it. And it's a picture of me, my mother, father, and my sister Nelly in-- in '39 in a spa in Krynica in Poland just before the outbreak of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the person on the left with the jacket on my arm. Yes, this a picture taken just probably just before we went into the ghetto or perhaps even in the ghetto. And it's a picture of my grandparents and my mother. And I'll-- I'll just describe who the people here. The person in the left lower corner is my Aunt Paula who was my father's sister, younger sister. And then in the first row it's my grandmother called Fela Blit. And then me and my grandfather, Wolf Blit. And then on the left side my twin sister Nelly and my mother. And then the small picture in-- is my mother when she was young, probably before I was born. This is a picture of my father, Lucjan Blit. And taken, I don't know, a long time after the end of the war. This is a pass, a school pass, just in-- in Lodz, at the end of the war just where I went to school and had this pass in a different name because I was still frightened, really, to be recognized that I was Jewish. I had this name Jasinska. That's the name and that's my picture. This-- this is an envelope of letter that my uncle had in America, which curiously reached him in '42 and sent out of the ghetto. And as you see, it says it has been opened by. I am not sure who by. And there are some German stamps on it. And inside, there was a letter written in my and my sister's and my cousin's childish handwriting. And bits have been cut out by the censors. I don't know what secrets that they worried about. This is a picture of Michal Klepfisz. When I knew him he was older, didn't quite look like that. He's the man who took us out of the ghetto and who went back to the ghetto when the uprising started and-- and was shot there. This is [INAUDIBLE] who was a courier and who came every month to pay for me whenever she could, and found families for me. This is my family, my husband Bruce, Susie, who's my middle daughter. Isabel is the eldest, and Mark is the youngest, so-- the youngest child. That's my granddaughter, Debbie, who's the daughter of Isabel. My grandson, Susie's sons, Danny and Alasdair. My sister, a picture mainly to show my sister Nelly, who's the third person on the left, and her husband [? Carl, ?] who now live in New York. And this is a plaque we put up in the Jewish cemetery in Warsaw this August, that's '94. Together with my cousins who now live in Paris, to remember the three families who were all killed.
